name: Theia
description: Theia, in Greek mythology, is a Titan goddess, often associated with sight, brilliance, and the shimmering light of the heavens. She is one of the twelve Titans, children of Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky), and therefore represents a primordial force of nature. Theia's name itself derives from the Greek word 'thea,' meaning 'goddess.' Her primary role lies in bestowing radiance and luminescence, and she is considered the mother of Helios (the Sun), Selene (the Moon), and Eos (the Dawn). Theia's significance extends beyond mere light; she embodies the clarity of vision and the insightful understanding that light can bring. She is also viewed as a source of prophecy and divine wisdom, capable of illuminating the hidden truths of the cosmos. Though not as prominently featured as some of her Olympian descendants, Theia remains a crucial figure in the Titan generation, representing a fundamental aspect of the natural world and the origins of celestial light.
subtitle: Goddess of Sight and Light
titles: Mother of the Sun, Moon, and Dawn

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Helios, Selene, and Eos
keyMyths.description: Theia's most significant myth revolves around her union with Hyperion, which resulted in the birth of Helios, Selene, and Eos. These children became vital celestial bodies, bringing light and order to the world. Helios, the sun, traverses the sky daily, while Selene, the moon, illuminates the night. Eos, the dawn, heralds the coming of each new day, painting the sky with vibrant colors. This myth highlights Theia's pivotal role in shaping the cosmos and establishing the rhythm of time.

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: As a Titan, Theia was involved in the Titanomachy, the epic battle between the Titans and the Olympian gods. While her specific actions during the war are not extensively detailed in surviving texts, it is understood that she fought alongside her siblings against the Olympians. The outcome of the Titanomachy led to the Titans' defeat and imprisonment in Tartarus, though it is unclear if Theia suffered the same fate as some of her brethren. Her participation underscores her connection to the older generation of gods and the struggle for cosmic dominance.

keyMyths.title: Theia's Association with Prophecy
keyMyths.description: Theia is sometimes linked to the concept of prophecy, due to her association with light and sight, which are often seen as metaphors for clarity and understanding. Her ability to perceive the world in its illuminated state allows her to see beyond the surface and into the deeper truths of existence. This connection to prophecy, though not explicitly stated in all accounts, positions Theia as a figure of wisdom and insight, capable of unveiling the hidden aspects of the future.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text for Greek mythology, detailing the origins of the gods and the cosmos.
sources.text: A foundational text for Greek mythology, detailing the origins of the gods and the cosmos.
